steps.title: Trace the passage's structure
steps.content: The passage moves in three beats: (1) it introduces Asteroid 6478 Gault's intermittent mass loss as a natural phenomenon, (2) it refutes two candidate explanations — solar energy–driven ice vaporization ("not attributable to") and impact ejection ("untenable") — and (3) it endorses a third explanation, rotational instability, as "likely correct." Hold that map — phenomenon, two refutations, one endorsed alternative — against the choices.

steps.title: Eliminate each choice
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘It presents a scientific observation, describes a contrast between that observation and other observations, and then explains why those other observations should not be considered credible.’: Misrepresents the structure: the passage refutes explanations for one phenomenon, not a contrast between multiple observations. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘It describes an astronomical finding, discusses competing theories about that finding that the author regards as flawed, and then describes new evidence that supports an alternative theory.’: Adds "new evidence" that never appears; the passage cites existing reasoning, not newly discovered data. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘It introduces a natural phenomenon, refutes two potential explanations for that phenomenon, and then presents a third explanation for that phenomenon that the author regards as plausible.’: Matches all three beats exactly: mass loss introduced, ice vaporization and impact ejection refuted, rotational instability endorsed as plausible. ✓
  • Choice D — ‘It discusses a physical process, evaluates possible causes of that process, and then states that a persuasive account of the process has yet to be put forward.’: Contradicts the passage: Luu et al.'s rotational-instability account is explicitly called "likely correct," so a persuasive account has been put forward. ✗
That confirms C is correct.
